hq: 124 City Road, London, United Kingdom, EC1V 2NX

system_description.overview: Distillery is an AI-automated end-to-end data infrastructure and analytics platform designed to help companies understand their customers. It automates data infrastructure setup, maintenance, and provides AI-driven insights allowing querying in English, with data health monitoring and access control.

products.Automated Data Infrastructure Management: End-to-end data infrastructure automation for fintech and other industries; maintenance-free and integrated with any raw data source; continuous data pipeline maintenance, monitoring and gap identification.
products.AI-Powered Data Infra and Analytics: Automated infrastructure setup and maintenance; AI-driven data insights and analytics allowing querying in English; detailed access control for users within client organisations.
products.Data Infrastructure Support Services: White-glove onboarding and implementation support; data infra setup, data source connections, pipeline setup and startup dashboard setup; ongoing data support and expert guidance.

infrastructure.cloud_provider: AWS (carve-out — AWS controls excluded from scope; Distillery relies on AWS SOC 2)
regions: Single region — region labelled 'Region #1' in network diagram; specific AWS region not named
infrastructure.physical_presence: None — no physical offices; all data processed on cloud services
infrastructure.availability_zones: Multi-AZ — 3 availability zones confirmed in network diagram (AZ #1, AZ #2, AZ #3)

network_architecture.network_segmentation: VPC with segmented subnets and ACLs. Each of 3 AZs has private and public subnets. Private subnets host EC2 instances (Prod). Public subnets host Prod Ingress (ALB/NLB), NAT Singleton (AZ2). Network ACLs applied to all subnets. VPC DHCP Options Assoc and DRProxy visible at VPC level.
network_architecture.api_gateway: Application Load Balancer / Network Load Balancer (Prod Ingress visible in network diagram across all 3 AZs)
network_architecture.waf: Not explicitly named; firewalls mentioned on application gateway and production network
network_architecture.firewalls: System firewalls configured on the application gateway and production network to limit unnecessary ports, protocols and services. Firewall rules reviewed annually. Firewall access restricted to authorised personnel.
network_architecture.ids_ips: IDS and IPS deployed via AWS to identify and protect against threats in conjunction with security policy network settings (p.18). Intrusion detection system used to monitor and detect unauthorised access or anomalies (CC2.1.1 confirmed no exceptions).
network_architecture.vpn: Bastion host with VPN for remote access (p.18)
network_architecture.tls: TLS using certificates from an established third-party certificate authority for all web-based system traffic
network_architecture.content_filtering: Proxy server-based content filtering; all systems restricted to internet access routed through proxy server (p.20)
network_architecture.topology: Internet traffic enters via EC2 Internet Gateway -> VPC -> Network ACL -> EC2 DHCP/Options/DRProxy layer -> distributed across 3 AZs each with public subnet (Prod Ingress ALB/NLB + EC2 NIC Group) and private subnet (Prod EC2 instances + EC2 NIC Group). AZ3 private subnet also contains 'Prod logstail-Buildlist'. DB instances (Prod DB, EC2 Volume Groups) visible per AZ. AWS SNS Topic Group, SQS Subnet Group, RDS Repository Group, and CloudTrail/DeadPoint/EventPolicy visible at region level.

application_architecture.pattern: Not explicitly disclosed — appears microservices or modular given multi-AZ EC2 deployment and dedicated ingress layer
compute: EC2 (primary compute, confirmed in network diagram across all AZs)
application_architecture.environments: Separate development/test and production environments confirmed (CC8.1.4, CC8.1.2 tested with no exceptions). Changes tested in dev/test before promotion to production.

databases: RDS (visible in network diagram as 'RDS Repository Group')
Prod DB (EC2-hosted database visible in AZ#1 private subnet)
file_storage: S3 implied via 'SNS Bucket Group' visible in network diagram
data_storage.data_warehouse: Not disclosed — product description implies data warehouse/analytics capabilities for customers but internal warehouse not named
data_storage.backup_frequency: Daily — CC7.4.2 confirms backups taken daily, multi-availability zones configured

authentication_access_control.identity_provider: AWS IAM / Active Directory (CC6.1.2 references Active Directory, server, and database accounts; passwords controlled through AWS Password policy)
authentication_access_control.mfa: MFA enforced for all user accounts with administrative access to production platform (CC6.1.1). MFA required for source code tool access (CC8.1.3). Two-factor authentication required for email access (p.17).
authentication_access_control.access_revocation_sla: Within one business day upon termination
authentication_access_control.password_policy: Controlled through AWS Password policy; periodic forced changes, password expiry and complexity requirements; accounts disabled after limited unsuccessful logon attempts; guest/anonymous logins not allowed; unattended desktops lock on inactivity

encryption.at_rest: Cloud provider key management service used to encrypt data at rest (CC6.1.5)
encryption.key_management: AWS KMS (cloud provider key management service referenced in CC6.1.5 and CC6.7)

ci_cd_devops.source_control: Not named — 'source code management tool' referenced; branch protection (merge to master requires approval) confirmed. Likely GitHub or GitLab.
ci_cd_devops.branch_protection: Code repository branch rules configured: every merge request to move change to production requires additional approval (CC8.1.2). Merging to master branch requires approval process (CC8.1.2).
ci_cd_devops.iac: Baseline configurations retained within infrastructure as code; configuration management tool used for implementation and rollback (CC7.1.1, CC8.1). Tool name not disclosed.
ci_cd_devops.container_orchestration: Not mentioned — EC2-based deployment implied from network diagram
ci_cd_devops.patch_management: CTO / Management has set up automatic patch management; monthly patch management reviews (ITLC meeting); workstations configured for automatic updates and scanned for patch compliance daily; OS patches on network devices managed automatically on renewal.

monitoring_logging.apm_tool: Not named — 'monitoring tool' generates alerts at predefined thresholds (CC5.2.1, CC5.2.1 tested no exceptions). Tool name not disclosed.
monitoring_logging.log_aggregation: Logging enabled at application and infrastructure level (administrative activities, logon attempts, data deletions, security configuration changes). 'Logstail-Buildlist' visible in AZ3 of network diagram — may indicate a log aggregation component. CloudTrail visible at region level.
monitoring_logging.alerting: Automated alerts configured to notify IT management of high and critical risk events; tracked via change-management/ticketing application

third_party_services.purpose: Cloud infrastructure and data center services (compute, storage, networking, database, KMS, IDS/IPS, VPC). Subservice organisation — carve-out method.

security_tools.antivirus: Endpoint Security Solution installed on all desktops and laptops; anti-virus software with automatic daily definition updates; continuous scanning on production servers with weekly definition updates; configured for Mac-based workstations and laptops (p.20, CC6.8.1, CC6.7)
security_tools.vulnerability_scanning: Monthly automated vulnerability scans on infrastructure and applications (CC7.1.2); internal IT Infrastructure team performs periodic scans per audit calendar; vulnerability scan prior to every production release or annually (p.17)
security_tools.penetration_testing: Annual external penetration testing by independent third party (CC4.1.1)

untestable: CC2.2.3 — No significant changes to people/roles during the engagement period; control could not be tested
CC3.2.1 (cybersecurity insurance) — No cyber incidents reported during engagement period; operating effectiveness could not be tested
CC6.5.2 — No customer terminations during engagement period; customer data deletion control could not be tested
CC7.3.1 — No security incidents reported during engagement period; incident response control could not be tested
excluded_criteria: CC6.4 — Physical access to data centers: AWS responsible for physical security; Distillery has no physical offices. Control not in scope.
A1.2 — Environmental protections: Distillery has no office; building management and AWS responsible for all environmental controls.
notable_controls: CC8.1.3 — Source code tool access restricted to authorised users with MFA; all changes logged, time-stamped, attributed to author
CC7.4.2 — Daily backups confirmed; multi-AZ configured; alerts in place for backup failures
CC6.2.2 — Quarterly user access reviews for all accounts including privileged accounts
CC7.1.2 — Monthly automated vulnerability scans on infrastructure and applications
CC4.1.1 — Annual external penetration test by independent third party
CC7.7 (CC7.1.1) — Baseline configurations retained as IaC with configuration management tool for rollback
CC3.2.1 — Cybersecurity insurance maintained (no incidents to test against during period)

infrastructure.rationale: Strong: single-region AWS with genuine 3-AZ architecture confirmed by detailed network diagram; private/public subnet segregation; VPC with ACLs; daily backups with multi-AZ; bastion host + VPN. Deductions: single region only (no multi-region), no CDN mentioned, specific AWS region not disclosed, no serverless or container orchestration visible.

application_architecture.rationale: Moderate: separate environments confirmed and tested; branch protection with required approvals tested; IaC for config management; source control with MFA-gated access. Deductions: no languages, frameworks, or architectural pattern disclosed; compute appears to be vanilla EC2 with no containers/ECS/EKS mentioned; no CI/CD tool named.

data_layer.rationale: Good: RDS + EC2-hosted DB visible; daily backups tested; multi-AZ confirmed; data classification policy in place; data retention policy confirmed; per-tenant segmentation confirmed (CC6.1.3). Deductions: no named caching layer; no named data warehouse (despite being an analytics platform); S3 inferred not explicit; backup/restore not explicitly tested.

security_posture.rationale: Good: IDS/IPS via AWS; annual external pen test; monthly vuln scans; endpoint AV on all devices; MFA for admin and email; firewall on app gateway and production network; proxy-based content filtering; AWS KMS encryption at rest; TLS in transit; cybersecurity insurance confirmed. Deductions: WAF not explicitly named; no SIEM named; no named security tooling vendor (all described generically); monitoring tool unnamed.

devops_maturity.rationale: Moderate: IaC confirmed (unnamed tool); branch protection with enforced approval for merges to master tested; separate environments; monthly patch reviews; automatic patch management. Deductions: CI/CD tool not named; source control platform not named; no containers/orchestration; no deployment frequency metrics; no named change management platform.

bcdr_readiness.rationale: Good: BCDR policy documented and reviewed annually; annual testing confirmed and tested with no exceptions; daily backups confirmed and tested; multi-AZ failover explicitly described (spinning up multiple servers across all AZs); incident response plan tested. Deductions: no RTO/RPO disclosed; no multi-region DR; RTO/RPO metrics absent from report entirely.

vendor_diversity.rationale: Weak: Only AWS named as a technology subservice provider. No other named cloud or SaaS vendors disclosed. Heavy single-cloud dependency on AWS with no named alternatives for any function. Monitoring tool, CI/CD, source control, authentication providers, and all other tooling unnamed — suggests either very thin stack or low disclosure. Significant concentration risk.

overall.rationale: Distillery presents a solid foundational security posture for an early-stage AI data infrastructure company: genuine 3-AZ AWS architecture with a detailed network diagram, no control exceptions over the 3-month period, clean pen test cadence, daily backups tested, and MFA enforced. However, the report is technically thin — no vendors named beyond AWS, no CI/CD or monitoring tool identified, no languages or frameworks disclosed, no RTO/RPO, and single-region only. The short 3-month audit period limits confidence. Suitable for initial due diligence; deeper technical questionnaire warranted.

red_flags: Extremely thin vendor disclosure — only AWS named as a technology provider; all other tooling (CI/CD, monitoring, source control, SIEM, identity provider) unnamed. Cannot assess toolchain risk or vendor lock-in beyond AWS.
Single region deployment — disaster recovery relies solely on multi-AZ within one AWS region; no cross-region failover capability described.
No RTO or RPO disclosed anywhere in the report despite BCDR policy and annual testing existing.
Very short audit period (3 months: April–July 2025) — limits confidence in operating effectiveness; multiple controls untestable due to no triggering events in the period.
No WAF explicitly named — firewalls on application gateway mentioned but no dedicated WAF service confirmed.
No SIEM named — log aggregation and alerting described generically without tool identification.
yellow_flags: Source control platform and CI/CD tool unnamed — branch protection and change controls tested successfully but toolchain opacity makes integration risk assessment difficult.
No container orchestration — compute appears to be EC2-only; no ECS, EKS, or Kubernetes mentioned, which may limit scalability and deployment agility.
Application architecture and tech stack entirely undisclosed — no languages, frameworks, or architectural pattern mentioned in a 80-page SOC 2 report is unusual.
Monitoring tool unnamed — alerting and threshold monitoring described and tested but no named APM/monitoring product (e.g., Datadog, CloudWatch) identified.
No caching layer mentioned — for a data analytics platform handling querying, absence of named caching raises performance architecture questions.
CEO is sole signing authority and described as overseeing 'all technological aspects' — key-person dependency risk for a small company.
green_flags: Genuine 3-AZ network diagram included — one of the more detailed infrastructure disclosures seen in Accorp-prepared reports; confirms real multi-AZ deployment.
Zero control exceptions across all 57 tested controls over the audit period.
Daily backups confirmed and tested with no exceptions; multi-AZ backup configuration tested and passed.
Annual external penetration testing by independent third party confirmed and tested.
Monthly automated vulnerability scanning on infrastructure and applications confirmed.
Quarterly user access reviews for all accounts including privileged accounts confirmed and tested.
Access revocation within one business day upon termination confirmed and tested.
MFA enforced for admin access, email, and source code tool access — broad MFA coverage.
IaC-based configuration management with rollback capability confirmed.
Per-tenant data segregation confirmed (CC6.1.3 tested with no exceptions).
Separate dev/test and production environments confirmed and tested.
No significant changes to the system during the audit period — stable platform.
key_observations: The network diagram (page 19) reveals a Kubernetes-like Pod-based EC2 architecture — labels show 'Pod', 'NetworkInterfaceGroup', 'Prod logstail-Buildlist', and 'Pod Ingress ALB' suggesting containerised workloads on EC2 even though ECS/EKS are not mentioned explicitly. This is worth probing.

Region #1 in the network diagram includes AWS-native services: CloudTrail, SNS Topic Group, SQS Subnet Group, RDS Repository Group, DeadPoint (likely Dead Letter Queue / SQS DLQ), and EventPolicy — this suggests an event-driven architecture using AWS-native messaging, which is materially more sophisticated than the generic description implies.

The 'DRProxy' component visible in the VPC layer of the network diagram is not described in the text — this may be a dedicated disaster recovery proxy or a data routing component and warrants clarification.

Distillery explicitly excludes Processing Integrity and Privacy trust criteria as 'not relevant to the system' — unusual for a platform that processes and analyses customer data on behalf of fintech companies. This is worth questioning in diligence.

The product sells AI-powered natural language querying over customer data ('querying in English') — the underlying LLM/AI infrastructure is entirely absent from the SOC 2 report, creating a significant blind spot for AI model risk, data leakage through AI, and third-party AI API dependencies.

No customer terminations, no security incidents, and no significant personnel changes occurred during the audit period — while positive, the 3-month window means most high-consequence controls were untestable and effectiveness evidence is thin.

The report was signed by the CEO (Vasil Yakov) on 8/28/2025 for a period ending 7/23/2025 — the 36-day gap between period end and management assertion is normal but the CEO directly signing (rather than a CISO or VP Engineering) reinforces key-person concentration.
